rationale: Curriculum phase 1 (LEARNING-SYSTEM §5). Reaction chunks come FIRST, before self-introduction, because 30 of these let you be a real presence at a table long before you can describe your job. They are also Levantine-general, so a Lebanese native speaker validates them almost perfectly — the one phase where the Palestinian/Lebanese delta barely applies.

chunks.use: surprise — the single most common reaction in Levantine
chunks.maknuune: UNSOURCED — و+الله strips to allah; wallah is not an entry

chunks.use: admiration — food, a view, a story

chunks.maknuune: UNSOURCED — only طبع 'quality' is an entry; the adverb is not

chunks.use: sympathy — THE sympathy phrase; use it constantly
brushing off a problem or apology
chunks.maknuune: UNSOURCED — not in Maknuune at all

chunks.use: parting, or sympathy for a hard road

chunks.maknuune: maknuune spells كتير as كثير (KTiir -> urban ktiir)

chunks.use: the most useful sentence for a beginner
discourse glue — starts half of all turns
the most-used filler in Arabic; buys thinking time
keeps a story going — makes you a good listener
hospitality; you will hear this every 90 seconds at dinner
said to someone eating; reply: على قلبك
chunks.maknuune: UNSOURCED — only صحة 'health'; the dual greeting is not an entry

chunks.use: TO THE COOK, after a meal. The single highest-value phrase at a Palestinian dinner table. Feminine ending — إيديك for a man.
chunks.maknuune: UNSOURCED — سلم is 'ladder'; the expression is not an entry

provenance_note: Chunks are CLAUDE-GENERATED, not Maknuune-sourced. Maknuune is a content lexicon — the interactional formulae that carry real conversation (معليش، والله، صحتين، يسلمو إيديك) are simply not in it, and its PHRASE entries are idioms, not conversational glue. So this deck's authority is a NATIVE SPEAKER, not the lexicon. Reaction chunks are Levantine-general, which is precisely where a Lebanese validator is near-perfect — the one phase where the Palestinian/Lebanese delta barely applies. Validate before shipping to audio.

spelling_notes.كتير: Maknuune/CODA spells this كثير (etymological). Kept as كتير — how Palestinians actually text it, and phonetically transparent for TTS. CODA كثير risks the voice reading MSA 'kathīr'.
spelling_notes.مظبوط: Maknuune spells مضبوط. Both circulate; kept mine pending native review.
spelling_notes.أيوه: Maknuune spells ايوا. Kept أيوه — the common written form.
